description: Common rhetorical rationalisations used to justify harmful acts. Recognition patterns only; documented in moral psychology and philosophy literature.

entries.description: Justifying harm or control over another person by claiming the action benefits them, without their informed consent.
keywords: I know what's best for you
you need this even if you don't see it
entries.source: Mill, On Liberty (1859): critique of paternalism. Paraphrased.

entries.description: Normalising harmful behaviour by claiming that its widespread occurrence removes moral responsibility.
Dismissing the harm caused by a remark or action by retroactively labelling it humour when the target objects.
keywords: you have no sense of humour
entries.source: Benign-violation theory (McGraw & Warren 2010); critical humour studies. Paraphrased.

entries.description: Using a desired outcome as justification to engage in actions that are otherwise clearly unethical.
entries.source: Kant: rejection of consequentialist excuse for rights violations. Paraphrased.

entries.description: Claiming the target of harm provoked or deserved the mistreatment, shifting moral blame to the victim.
Describing a person or group using language that strips them of human qualities to make harm against them feel more acceptable.
keywords: they don't feel pain like us

entries.description: Presenting a chain of speculative consequences to argue that a minor concession will inevitably lead to catastrophic outcomes, used to manipulate through fear.
keywords: the thin end of the wedge

entries.description: Invoking a sacred, ideological, or patriotic cause to bypass the individual ethical evaluation of a harmful act.
entries.source: Bandura (1999) sanctioned imperatives; Arendt (1963) on ideological justification. Paraphrased.
